title: Find the Processes Burning CPU
hook: A server feels slow, but you need proof before restarting anything.
problem: You need to identify which processes are using the most CPU without changing system state.
starting_state: A Linux server feels slow and you need a point-in-time process snapshot.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads the process table and sorts by CPU use.
when_to_use: Use when load is high or the machine feels slow and you need to find CPU-heavy processes.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as the only signal for long-term performance diagnosis; pair it with uptime and memory checks.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A sorted process list with the highest CPU consumers near the top.

shorts.title: Find CPU burners fast.
shorts.voiceover: Before restarting services, sort the process table by CPU and identify the actual noisy process.
shorts.caption: Sort ps by CPU before reacting.

linkedin.question: What is your first proof point before restarting a slow service?
linkedin.cta: Start with a read-only CPU snapshot before changing state.

youtube.description: Use ps sorted by CPU to identify high-CPU Linux processes safely.

title: Find the Processes Eating Memory
hook: Memory pressure can look like a slow app, a stuck deploy, or random crashes.
problem: You need to see which processes are using the most RAM.
starting_state: A Linux server may be low on memory or swapping.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads process metadata and sorts by memory percentage.
when_to_use: Use when memory is low, swapping is suspected, or an app may be leaking memory.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to estimate total memory health by itself; check free -h too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A process list sorted by memory percentage, with RSS shown for each process.

shorts.title: Find memory-heavy processes.
shorts.voiceover: Low memory needs evidence. Sort ps by memory and include RSS so the largest resident processes stand out.
shorts.caption: Sort ps by memory before guessing.

linkedin.question: When memory is tight, do you inspect total memory first or top processes first?
linkedin.cta: Use both: free for pressure, ps for suspects.

youtube.description: Use ps sorted by memory to find large resident processes.

title: Check Memory Pressure with free
hook: Linux memory numbers look scary until you know which column matters.
problem: You need to tell whether the system is actually low on available memory.
starting_state: A Linux server is slow or processes may be getting killed.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command displays memory and swap totals.
when_to_use: Use when apps are slow, processes are being killed, or memory pressure is suspected.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat the free column alone as the truth; Linux uses memory for cache.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A memory summary showing total, used, free, buff/cache, available, and swap.

shorts.title: Read Linux memory correctly.
shorts.voiceover: In free, start with available, not panic. Cache is normal; low available plus swap use is the warning.
shorts.caption: Look at available memory first.

linkedin.question: Which free column do you check first during memory triage?
linkedin.cta: Treat available memory as the first quick read.

youtube.description: Read free -h output and focus on available memory and swap.

title: Read Load Average Before You React
hook: A high load number is a clue, not a diagnosis.
problem: You need a fast snapshot of system load and how long the machine has been running.
starting_state: A Linux server may be under sustained resource pressure.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints uptime, users, and load averages.
when_to_use: Use at the start of performance triage to see whether pressure is rising or falling.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume high load always means CPU saturation; blocked I/O can raise load too.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A single line showing current time, uptime, user count, and 1, 5, and 15 minute load averages.

shorts.title: Read server pressure fast.
shorts.voiceover: uptime gives the one, five, and fifteen minute load averages so you can see whether pressure is new or sustained.
shorts.caption: Load average is a clue.

linkedin.question: Do you read load average as a trend or a single number?
linkedin.cta: Compare the 1, 5, and 15 minute values before reacting.

youtube.description: Use uptime to read Linux load averages as a first triage signal.

title: Check Filesystem Space with df
hook: A full disk can break logins, uploads, databases, and deploys.
problem: You need to see which mounted filesystem is low on space.
starting_state: Writes are failing or a service reports no space left on device.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reports mounted filesystem usage.
when_to_use: Use when writes fail, logs stop, uploads break, or services report no space left on device.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to find the specific large directory; use du for that.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A table of filesystems showing size, used space, available space, use percentage, and mount point.

shorts.title: Which filesystem is full?
shorts.voiceover: df answers the first disk question: which mounted filesystem is out of room?
shorts.caption: Start disk triage with df -h.

linkedin.question: What usually fills first on your VPS: logs, uploads, cache, or database files?
linkedin.cta: Use df first, then narrow with du.

youtube.description: Use df -h to identify full Linux filesystems before digging into directories.

title: Check Inodes When Disk Space Looks Fine
hook: Sometimes the disk has free bytes but still cannot create files.
problem: You need to check whether a filesystem has run out of inodes.
starting_state: File creation fails even though normal disk space looks available.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reports inode availability by filesystem.
when_to_use: Use when file creation fails even though df -h shows available space.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to locate the directory with too many files; follow up with targeted directory inspection.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A filesystem table showing inode totals, used inodes, free inodes, inode use percentage, and mount points.

shorts.title: No space, but free GB?
shorts.voiceover: If bytes are available but files still cannot be created, check inode usage with df -ih.
shorts.caption: Disk space is bytes and inodes.

linkedin.question: Have you hit inode exhaustion on a server that still had free GB?
linkedin.cta: Add df -ih to your disk triage checklist.

youtube.description: Use df -ih to catch inode exhaustion when byte usage looks fine.

experiments.variant_b: No space left can mean inodes.

title: Find Large Directories with du
hook: Once you know a filesystem is full, the next question is where.
problem: You need to identify which top-level directory is using the most space.
starting_state: df shows a full filesystem and you have narrowed the likely path.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command measures directory sizes and sorts them.
when_to_use: Use after df -h shows a full filesystem and you need to locate the largest directory.
when_not_to_use: Do not start at / on a busy production system without narrowing the path first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A size-sorted list of directories under /var, with the largest entries at the bottom.

shorts.title: df says full. du says where.
shorts.voiceover: After df identifies the full filesystem, use a targeted du scan to find the large directory.
shorts.caption: Keep du scoped and sorted.

linkedin.question: Do you start disk hunts at /, or narrow the path first?
linkedin.cta: Keep recursive size scans scoped during incidents.

youtube.description: Use du with max-depth to find large directories under a known path.

title: Find Listening Ports with ss
hook: Before blaming the firewall, check whether anything is actually listening.
problem: You need to list local TCP services that are accepting connections.
starting_state: A service should be reachable or you need to audit listening ports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command displays listening TCP sockets.
when_to_use: Use when a service should be reachable, or when you need to audit listening ports.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume a listening socket is externally reachable; firewall and bind address still matter.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A table of listening TCP sockets with local addresses, ports, and associated processes when available.

shorts.title: What is actually listening?
shorts.voiceover: ss shows local listening sockets, bind addresses, ports, and processes before you chase firewall rules.
shorts.caption: Reachability starts with listening sockets.

linkedin.question: When a service is unreachable, do you check bind address before firewall rules?
linkedin.cta: Use ss to prove what is listening locally.

youtube.description: Use ss -ltnp to inspect listening TCP ports and processes.

title: Find Open Deleted Files with lsof
hook: A file can be deleted but still occupy disk while a process holds it open.
problem: You need to spot deleted files still held by running processes after cleanup.
starting_state: Disk usage stayed high after deleting large logs or temporary files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists open files with link counts below one.
when_to_use: Use when disk space remains used after deleting logs or temporary files.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a cleanup action; it only identifies processes and files.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A list of open deleted files, including command, PID, file descriptor, size, and path.

shorts.title: Deleted logs still using disk?
shorts.voiceover: If space did not return after deleting a huge file, a process may still hold it open. lsof +L1 shows it.
shorts.caption: Find open deleted files.

linkedin.question: Have you ever deleted logs and wondered why disk space did not come back?
linkedin.cta: Check for open deleted files before restarting services blindly.

youtube.description: Use lsof +L1 to find deleted files still held open by processes.

experiments.variant_b: Find what holds deleted logs open.

title: Inspect Established Network Connections
hook: Unexpected connections are easier to reason about when you can see them directly.
problem: You need to list established TCP connections without changing network state.
starting_state: You need a point-in-time view of active TCP conversations.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command displays current established TCP sockets.
when_to_use: Use when checking active client connections, suspicious network activity, or service traffic.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat it as a full security investigation; it is a point-in-time socket snapshot.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A list of established TCP connections showing local and peer addresses and ports.

shorts.title: See active TCP conversations.
shorts.voiceover: Filter ss to established sockets when you need to see who the server is actively talking to.
shorts.caption: Established sockets are a snapshot.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect established sockets during incident timelines?
linkedin.cta: Use ss as a quick snapshot, then move to logs for history.

youtube.description: Use ss to list established TCP connections during triage.
